title: New England’s most patriotic summer getaways
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: Forbes Travel Guide highlights all-American getaways in New England for the nation's 250th birthday.
Boston offers numerous free June events, including outdoor workouts, live music, and World Cup parties.
Car camping popularity rises in Massachusetts, facilitated by platforms like Hipcamp.

title: 189-square-foot Cape Cod beach cottage lists for $295k
author: Megan Johnson
bullets: A 189-square-foot cottage in Truro is on sale for $295,000, offering a one-bedroom and one-bathroom layout.
Located at 321 Shore Road, Unit 16S, the cottage includes deeded beach access and two parking spots, with a $60 monthly HOA fee.
Built in 1940, this seasonal home is part of the White Village Condo Association and features views of the bay and dunes.

title: Recent homes sales in Greater Boston (June 3)
author: The Boston Globe
bullets: A Cape Cod home in Boston sold for $21 million, making it one of the priciest in the area.
In Arlington, a one-family ranch built in 1951 sold for $1,630,000 on a 16,278-square-foot lot.
An Allston condo low-rise, built in 2002, sold for $1,070,000 with 1,985 square feet and three bedrooms.

title: Wu says she’s secured commitments for nearly 500 teen afterschool jobs, is seeking 1500 more
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Mayor Michelle Wu secured commitments for nearly 500 part-time afterschool jobs for Boston students via private partnerships.
Wu aims for 2,000 part-time afterschool jobs as the city cuts over $5 million in youth employment program funding.
Organizations like the YMCA and Boys & Girls Clubs pledge to hire 475 Boston teens for the upcoming school year.

title: Red Sox veteran has interesting response about Boston’s Fenway struggles
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: The Boston Red Sox are struggling at Fenway Park, holding a 9-20 home record in the 2026 season.
Isiah Kiner-Falefa cited a different atmosphere at home, attributing struggles to more distractions and less team cohesion.
The Red Sox average 3.2 runs per game at Fenway, compared to 4.7 on the road, affecting their performance.

title: Bruins might have some tough decisions to make about Pavel Zacha this summer
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Pavel Zacha had a career year with the Bruins, scoring 30 goals and 65 points in the 2025-26 season.
Zacha's contract runs through 2026-27 with a cap hit of $4.75 million, but he is due for a significant pay raise.
Boston may consider trading Zacha, using him as a trade chip to gain draft capital or fill other roster needs.

title: Berkshire County man charged with murder after body found in greenhouse
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Cole Bushnell, 41, of Sheffield, charged with murder after allegedly killing Michael Moore, 40, and hiding body in greenhouse.
Bushnell was arraigned in Great Barrington District Court, held without bail, and faces a no-contact order for witnesses.
Bushnell and Moore, both painters, had a conflict over a shared job before the alleged murder occurred.

title: Pete Alonso homers for Orioles as Baltimore tops Red Sox 4-2
author: The Associated Press
bullets: Pete Alonso hit a two-run homer, leading the Baltimore Orioles to a 4-2 victory over the Boston Red Sox.
Orioles' Shane Baz pitched seven innings, allowing four hits and striking out six, securing his third win.
Wilyer Abreu and Marcelo Mayer's sacrifice flies accounted for the Red Sox's two runs at Fenway Park.

title: Mass. sheriffs’ offices run finances ‘like the wild west,’ Inspector General says
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Inspector General report reveals financial mismanagement at Massachusetts sheriffs' offices, citing overspending and lack of oversight.
Sheriffs' offices recorded a combined $110 million deficit in FY25 due to unregulated private accounts and misallocated funds.
The OIG recommends standardizing practices in sheriffs' offices to better manage public funds and address financial issues.

title: Hampshire College warns final-year students may not be able to receive their degrees after all
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Hampshire College announced it might not afford the planned teach-out for final-year students after the fall 2026 closure.
President Jennifer Chrisler stated the college lacks funds to complete degree programs, urging efforts to secure financial resources.
Declining enrollment and financial issues prompted Hampshire's closure, amid scrutiny from the New England Commission of Higher Education.
